instruction: Explain custom deleters with smart pointers in C++. When do you need them and what are the patterns?
output:
<think>
Custom deleters tell smart pointers HOW to release a resource when it's no longer needed. By default, unique_ptr calls delete and shared_ptr calls delete, but many resources aren't allocated with new: C library handles (fopen/fclose, SDL, OpenGL), Win32 handles, mapped memory, pool-allocated objects. Custom deleters let you wrap ANY resource with RAII semantics. For unique_ptr, the deleter is part of the type (zero overhead if it's a stateless functor or function pointer). For shared_ptr, the deleter is type-erased (stored in the control block), so different shared_ptrs to the same type can have different deleters. This makes shared_ptr more flexible but slightly heavier. Lambda deleters are the most convenient. A common pattern is wrapping C APIs: create the resource, wrap in unique_ptr with custom deleter, and the resource is automatically cleaned up on scope exit or exception.
</think>

```cpp
#include <cstdio>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <functional>

// === FILE HANDLE with custom deleter ===

// Lambda deleter (most common)
auto make_file(const char* path, const char* mode) {
    FILE* f = std::fopen(path, mode);
    if (!f) throw std::runtime_error("Failed to open file");

    // unique_ptr with lambda deleter — auto-closes on scope exit
    return std::unique_ptr<FILE, decltype([](FILE* fp) {
        if (fp) {
            std::cout << "Closing file\n";
            std::fclose(fp);
        }
    })>(f);
}

// Function pointer deleter (C++17 compatible)
struct FileDeleter {
    void operator()(FILE* fp) const {
        if (fp) std::fclose(fp);
    }
};

using FilePtr = std::unique_ptr<FILE, FileDeleter>;

// === C LIBRARY WRAPPER ===

// Simulating a C library with create/destroy functions
struct CHandle { int id; };
CHandle* c_create_handle(int id) {
    auto h = new CHandle{id};
    std::cout << "C handle " << id << " created\n";
    return h;
}
void c_destroy_handle(CHandle* h) {
    std::cout << "C handle " << h->id << " destroyed\n";
    delete h;
}

// Wrap with unique_ptr + custom deleter
auto make_handle(int id) {
    return std::unique_ptr<CHandle, decltype(&c_destroy_handle)>(
        c_create_handle(id),
        c_destroy_handle
    );
}

// === shared_ptr: Deleter is type-erased ===

void shared_ptr_deleters() {
    // Different deleters, same type!
    std::shared_ptr<int> heap(new int(42));  // Default delete

    int stack_val = 99;
    std::shared_ptr<int> no_delete(&stack_val, [](int*) {
        // No-op deleter — don't delete stack memory!
        std::cout << "Not deleting stack value\n";
    });

    // Both are shared_ptr<int> — can be stored in same container
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<int>> ptrs = {heap, no_delete};
}

// === ARRAY with custom deleter ===

// unique_ptr<T[]> has built-in array delete[]
auto arr1 = std::make_unique<int[]>(100);  // Uses delete[]

// shared_ptr with array (pre-C++20)
std::shared_ptr<int> arr2(new int[100], std::default_delete<int[]>());

// C++20: std::make_shared<int[]>(100)

// === PRACTICAL: Connection pool with return-to-pool deleter ===

class ConnectionPool {
public:
    struct Connection {
        int fd;
        bool valid;
    };

    std::unique_ptr<Connection, std::function<void(Connection*)>>
    acquire() {
        auto conn = new Connection{next_fd_++, true};
        std::cout << "Acquired connection " << conn->fd << "\n";

        // Custom deleter returns to pool instead of destroying
        return {conn, [this](Connection* c) {
            std::cout << "Returning connection " << c->fd << " to pool\n";
            // In real code: mark as available, don't delete
            delete c;  // Simplified
        }};
    }

private:
    int next_fd_ = 100;
};

int main() {
    // File handle — auto-closed
    {
        FilePtr f(std::fopen("/dev/null", "w"));
        if (f) std::fputs("hello", f.get());
    } // fclose called automatically

    // C handle — auto-destroyed
    {
        auto h = make_handle(42);
    } // c_destroy_handle called

    // Pool connection — returned on scope exit
    ConnectionPool pool;
    {
        auto conn = pool.acquire();
        // Use connection...
    } // Returned to pool automatically
}
```

instruction: Explain enable_shared_from_this in C++ and the aliasing constructor of shared_ptr. When do you need each?
output:
<think>
enable_shared_from_this solves a specific problem: when an object managed by shared_ptr needs to give out additional shared_ptrs to itself. You can't just create a new shared_ptr from 'this' — that would create a second control block, leading to double-free. enable_shared_from_this provides shared_from_this() which returns a shared_ptr that shares ownership with the existing one. It works by storing a weak_ptr internally that gets initialized when the first shared_ptr is created. The aliasing constructor is a different feature: it creates a shared_ptr that shares ownership with one object but points to a different object (like a member). The shared_ptr keeps the parent alive while providing a pointer to the member. This is useful for returning shared access to a component without the overhead of managing the component's lifetime separately. Both features are about controlling shared ownership semantics precisely.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <vector>

// === ENABLE_SHARED_FROM_THIS ===
// Problem: An object managed by shared_ptr needs to hand out
// additional shared_ptrs to itself (e.g., for callbacks, registries)

class Session : public std::enable_shared_from_this<Session> {
public:
    static std::shared_ptr<Session> create(int id) {
        // Can't use make_shared with private constructor
        return std::shared_ptr<Session>(new Session(id));
    }

    void start() {
        // ✅ shared_from_this() returns shared_ptr sharing ownership
        auto self = shared_from_this();

        // Register callback that keeps session alive
        register_callback([self]() {
            std::cout << "Callback for session " << self->id_ << "\n";
        });

        // ❌ BAD: Would create SECOND control block → double free!
        // auto bad_ptr = std::shared_ptr<Session>(this);
    }

    ~Session() {
        std::cout << "Session " << id_ << " destroyed\n";
    }

private:
    Session(int id) : id_(id) {}
    int id_;

    static void register_callback(std::function<void()> cb) {
        cb(); // Simplified — real code would store it
    }
};

// === ALIASING CONSTRUCTOR ===
// shared_ptr that points to a member but keeps the parent alive

struct Engine {
    int horsepower = 300;
};

struct Car {
    std::string model;
    Engine engine;

    Car(std::string m, int hp) : model(std::move(m)) {
        engine.horsepower = hp;
    }

    ~Car() {
        std::cout << "Car " << model << " destroyed\n";
    }
};

std::shared_ptr<Engine> get_engine(std::shared_ptr<Car> car) {
    // Aliasing constructor: shares ownership with car,
    // but points to car->engine
    return std::shared_ptr<Engine>(car, &car->engine);

    // The Engine pointer is valid as long as ANY shared_ptr
    // in the ownership group exists (including this one)
}

// === PRACTICAL: Observable with shared_from_this ===

class Observer {
public:
    virtual void notify(const std::string& event) = 0;
    virtual ~Observer() = default;
};

class EventBus {
public:
    void subscribe(std::weak_ptr<Observer> observer) {
        observers_.push_back(std::move(observer));
    }

    void publish(const std::string& event) {
        // Clean expired observers and notify active ones
        auto it = observers_.begin();
        while (it != observers_.end()) {
            if (auto obs = it->lock()) {
                obs->notify(event);
                ++it;
            } else {
                it = observers_.erase(it); // Observer was destroyed
            }
        }
    }

private:
    std::vector<std::weak_ptr<Observer>> observers_;
};

class Logger : public Observer, public std::enable_shared_from_this<Logger> {
public:
    void register_with(EventBus& bus) {
        // Give weak_ptr of ourselves — bus doesn't extend our lifetime
        bus.subscribe(shared_from_this());
    }

    void notify(const std::string& event) override {
        std::cout << "[LOG] " << event << "\n";
    }
};

int main() {
    // enable_shared_from_this
    {
        auto session = Session::create(1);
        session->start();  // Uses shared_from_this internally
    } // Session destroyed after all shared_ptrs gone

    // Aliasing constructor
    {
        auto car = std::make_shared<Car>("Tesla", 450);
        auto engine = get_engine(car);
        car.reset(); // Release our reference to car

        // Car is STILL ALIVE because engine shares ownership
        std::cout << "Engine HP: " << engine->horsepower << "\n"; // Safe!
    } // Now car is destroyed (engine was last owner)

    // Observable pattern
    EventBus bus;
    {
        auto logger = std::make_shared<Logger>();
        logger->register_with(bus);
        bus.publish("user.login");  // Logger notified
    } // Logger destroyed
    bus.publish("user.logout");     // Logger skipped (weak_ptr expired)
}
```

instruction: When should you use weak_ptr in C++? Show the circular reference problem and observer pattern.
output:
<think>
weak_ptr exists to break circular references and to observe without owning. The circular reference problem: if object A has a shared_ptr to B and B has a shared_ptr to A, neither will ever be destroyed because each keeps the other's reference count above zero. Replacing one of the shared_ptrs with a weak_ptr breaks the cycle. weak_ptr doesn't increment the reference count, so it doesn't prevent destruction. Before using a weak_ptr, you must call lock() which returns a shared_ptr — either valid (if the object still exists) or null (if it's been destroyed). This makes weak_ptr perfect for: caches (hold weak references to cached objects, letting them be freed when not in use), observer patterns (observers don't keep subjects alive), parent pointers in tree structures (children own parent relationship but don't extend parent lifetime), and any situation where you need a non-owning reference to a shared object.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <vector>

// === CIRCULAR REFERENCE PROBLEM ===

struct Node {
    std::string name;
    std::shared_ptr<Node> next;   // Owns next node
    // std::shared_ptr<Node> prev; // ❌ Circular! Neither node freed
    std::weak_ptr<Node> prev;     // ✅ Non-owning back-reference

    Node(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {
        std::cout << name << " created\n";
    }
    ~Node() {
        std::cout << name << " destroyed\n";
    }
};

void circular_demo() {
    auto a = std::make_shared<Node>("A");
    auto b = std::make_shared<Node>("B");

    a->next = b;        // A → B (shared_ptr)
    b->prev = a;        // B → A (weak_ptr — doesn't prevent A's destruction)

    // Access via weak_ptr — must lock() first
    if (auto prev = b->prev.lock()) {
        std::cout << "B's prev is: " << prev->name << "\n";
    }
} // Both A and B are properly destroyed

// === CACHE: Hold weak references, let objects be freed ===

class ImageCache {
public:
    std::shared_ptr<std::string> get(const std::string& path) {
        // Check if cached image is still alive
        auto it = cache_.find(path);
        if (it != cache_.end()) {
            if (auto img = it->second.lock()) {
                std::cout << "Cache hit: " << path << "\n";
                return img;  // Still alive — return shared_ptr
            }
            cache_.erase(it);  // Expired — clean up
        }

        // Load image (simplified as string)
        std::cout << "Cache miss: " << path << " (loading)\n";
        auto img = std::make_shared<std::string>("[image data: " + path + "]");
        cache_[path] = img;  // Store weak_ptr — doesn't prevent freeing
        return img;
    }

private:
    std::unordered_map<std::string, std::weak_ptr<std::string>> cache_;
};

// === OBSERVER PATTERN with weak_ptr ===

class Subject {
public:
    using Callback = std::function<void(int)>;

    // Subscribe returns an ID; stores weak reference
    void subscribe(std::shared_ptr<Callback> callback) {
        observers_.push_back(callback);
    }

    void notify(int value) {
        auto it = observers_.begin();
        while (it != observers_.end()) {
            if (auto cb = it->lock()) {
                (*cb)(value);  // Observer still alive
                ++it;
            } else {
                it = observers_.erase(it);  // Observer gone
            }
        }
    }

private:
    std::vector<std::weak_ptr<Callback>> observers_;
};

int main() {
    // Circular reference fix
    circular_demo();

    // Cache demo
    ImageCache cache;
    {
        auto img = cache.get("photo.jpg");  // Cache miss
        auto img2 = cache.get("photo.jpg"); // Cache hit
    } // img released — cache holds expired weak_ptr
    auto img3 = cache.get("photo.jpg");     // Cache miss (was freed)

    // Observer demo
    Subject subject;
    {
        auto handler = std::make_shared<Subject::Callback>(
            [](int v) { std::cout << "Observer got: " << v << "\n"; }
        );
        subject.subscribe(handler);
        subject.notify(42);  // Observer notified
    } // handler destroyed
    subject.notify(99);      // Observer auto-removed (weak_ptr expired)
}
```

instruction: Compare make_unique vs raw new in C++, and explain when make_shared is better than shared_ptr constructor. What about the aliasing constructor?
output:
<think>
make_unique and make_shared are almost always preferred over raw new for three reasons: exception safety, performance, and clarity. Exception safety: in f(shared_ptr<A>(new A), g()), if new A succeeds but g() throws, the A object leaks because the shared_ptr was never constructed. make_shared avoids this by combining allocation and construction atomically. Performance: make_shared allocates the object AND the control block in a single allocation (one malloc vs two), improving cache locality and reducing allocator overhead. Clarity: make_unique makes ownership explicit and eliminates the visual asymmetry of new without delete. The cases where you CAN'T use make_shared: custom deleters (make_shared always uses default delete), weak_ptr lifetime extension (make_shared's single allocation means the memory stays allocated until all weak_ptrs are gone too), and private constructors (make_shared can't access them without friend declaration). make_unique has fewer restrictions — the main one is custom deleters and array types before C++20.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

// === make_unique: Always prefer over raw new ===

struct Widget {
    std::string name;
    int value;
    Widget(std::string n, int v) : name(std::move(n)), value(v) {
        std::cout << "Widget created: " << name << "\n";
    }
    ~Widget() { std::cout << "Widget destroyed: " << name << "\n"; }
};

void demo_make_unique() {
    // ✅ GOOD: make_unique
    auto w1 = std::make_unique<Widget>("modern", 42);

    // ❌ BAD: Raw new — exception safety risk
    // std::unique_ptr<Widget> w2(new Widget("old", 1));
    // In expressions like: process(unique_ptr<A>(new A), mayThrow())
    // If mayThrow() throws after new A but before unique_ptr construction → leak!

    // make_unique with arrays (C++14)
    auto arr = std::make_unique<int[]>(100);  // 100 ints, value-initialized to 0

    // When you CAN'T use make_unique: custom deleter
    auto file = std::unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(&fclose)>(
        fopen("/dev/null", "w"), fclose
    );
}

// === make_shared: Single allocation optimization ===

void demo_make_shared() {
    // ✅ GOOD: make_shared — ONE allocation for object + control block
    auto w1 = std::make_shared<Widget>("efficient", 1);
    // Memory layout: [ref_count | weak_count | Widget data]
    //                 ^-- single allocation

    // ❌ LESS GOOD: Two allocations
    std::shared_ptr<Widget> w2(new Widget("two_allocs", 2));
    // Memory: [Widget data] ← allocation 1
    //         [ref_count | weak_count | ptr] ← allocation 2

    // When make_shared HURTS: weak_ptr lifetime extension
    // With make_shared, the ENTIRE allocation (object + control block)
    // stays alive until ALL weak_ptrs are gone.
    // With separate allocation, the object memory is freed when the
    // last shared_ptr dies, even if weak_ptrs remain.

    std::weak_ptr<Widget> weak;
    {
        auto shared = std::make_shared<Widget>("stays", 3);
        weak = shared;
    } // Widget destructor runs, BUT memory stays allocated!
    // weak.expired() == true, but underlying memory not freed
    // until weak is destroyed or reset.
}

// === When you CAN'T use make_shared ===

class Restricted {
public:
    static std::shared_ptr<Restricted> create(int id) {
        // Can't use make_shared — constructor is private
        // make_shared<Restricted>(id) won't compile
        return std::shared_ptr<Restricted>(new Restricted(id));
    }

private:
    Restricted(int id) : id_(id) {}
    int id_;
};

// Custom deleter — must use constructor
auto pool_object = std::shared_ptr<Widget>(
    new Widget("pooled", 99),
    [](Widget* w) {
        std::cout << "Returning to pool: " << w->name << "\n";
        delete w;
    }
);

// === COMPARISON TABLE ===
// 
// Feature           | make_unique/make_shared | raw new + constructor
// ------------------|------------------------|----------------------
// Exception safety  | ✅ Guaranteed           | ❌ Risk in expressions
// Allocations       | 1 (make_shared)         | 2 (object + control)
// Custom deleter    | ❌ Not supported        | ✅ Supported
// Private ctor      | ❌ Needs friend         | ✅ Works
// weak_ptr memory   | ⚠️ Delayed free         | ✅ Freed with last shared
// Readability       | ✅ Clear intent         | ❌ Asymmetric new/no-delete

int main() {
    demo_make_unique();
    demo_make_shared();
}
```
